items.q: If air resistance is ignored, which falls faster: a heavy ball or a light ball dropped from the same height?
items.correct: Both fall at the same rate because gravitational acceleration is independent of mass.
items.misconception: Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects because gravity pulls more on them.
items.random_wrong: The lighter ball falls faster because it has less inertia.
items.why: Students reason 'more gravity force = more acceleration' but forget the heavier object also has proportionally more inertia, cancelling out.

items.q: Is the cosmological redshift of distant galaxies a true Doppler shift?
items.correct: No — it arises from the expansion of space stretching photon wavelengths during transit, not from the galaxy's motion through space.
items.misconception: Yes — it is the same Doppler shift as a moving siren on Earth.
items.random_wrong: No — it is caused by dust in space absorbing blue light.
items.why: Students learn the Doppler analogy early and don't update to the more accurate metric-expansion picture in cosmology.

items.q: In what direction does a magnetic field exert a force on a current-carrying wire?
items.correct: Perpendicular to both the current direction and the magnetic field direction (Fleming's left hand rule).
items.misconception: The force is in the same direction as the magnetic field.
items.random_wrong: The force is in the same direction as the current in the wire.
items.why: Students expect forces to act 'along' visible directions; the perpendicular result is counterintuitive without a vector cross product mental model.

items.q: Redshift of distant galaxies is evidence that:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: the universe is expanding. “the universe is contracting” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The Sun is currently in which stage?
items.why: Correct answer: Main sequence. “Protostar” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If the universe is 13.8 billion years old, how many seconds is that? (1 year ≈ 3.15×10⁷ s; answer in 10¹⁷ s)
items.why: Exact answer: 4.35 x10^17 s. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
